headline: Educators converge on the agent harness: the runtime scaffolding around models, from interruptible voice pipelines to memory layers, is where this cycle's teaching lands.
body: The recommended thread is production agent infrastructure beyond the model: Better Stack walks through TEN Framework, an open-source system for real-time voice agents that handles interruptions, VAD, turn detection, and STT-LLM-TTS pipelines via graph-based extensions [0]. That framing extends across the AI Engineer talks, where Isadora Martin-Dye layers brand voice into four tiers including a deterministic post-generation veto [4], and Paul Iusztin and Louis-François Bouchard convert 10,994 notes into queryable agent memory with a three-layer architecture that skips vector and graph databases [12]. Google Cloud reinforces the point by enumerating five components an agent harness needs to move from demo to deployment [9], while AICodeKing covers Ornith, DeepReinforce's MIT-licensed agentic coding models (9B-397B) for fully local tool use [13]. On the industry side, Dwarkesh Patel maps the next training paradigm around RLVR, grindability, verifiability, and test-time scaling [8], and Alex Kantrowitz's summit centers a federal recall of Anthropic's Fable and Mythos models alongside the compute race [7].

cited_items.title: TEN Framework: Voice AI That Can Actually Be Interrupted
